policy_statement: Develop City of Austin design and construction specifications and purchasing agreements for healthy, low-carbon buildings in partnership with other cities and states.
mechanism_description: Adopting low-carbon building specifications and purchasing standards reduces the embodied emissions associated with construction materials and building delivery.
typology_evidence_quote: develop City of Austin design and construction specifications and purchasing agreements to result in healthy, low-carbon buildings
primary_causal_pathway: Building design/procurement/material choice → ↓ embodied carbon in materials and construction → ↓ lifecycle GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) The policy changes human decisions about what materials are specified, purchased, and used in building construction, and may also encourage design optimization that uses less carbon-intensive or lower-volume materials. (2) The mediating physical process is reduced upstream and onsite emissions from raw material extraction, industrial processing, manufacturing, transport, and construction activities; for example, using low-clinker cement, recycled steel, timber in appropriate applications, or material-efficient structural design lowers emissions released per unit of building delivered. (3) The climate-relevant outcome is lower embodied lifecycle greenhouse gas emissions associated with new buildings and major renovations.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from the production and use of construction materials, thereby lowering embodied emissions from buildings.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1. Dominant causal pathway: the mechanism primarily lowers GHG emissions by reducing the carbon intensity of construction materials and building delivery.  
2. Why this primary over alternatives: although some policies may also reduce raw material use, the defining mechanism here is not merely using fewer resources for its own sake but specifically lowering embodied carbon, which makes the dominant outcome emissions mitigation. It is not Adaptation because it does not primarily reduce exposure or vulnerability to climate hazards. It is not Nature-Based Solutions because any use of bio-based materials is incidental unless the mechanism is centered on ecosystem enhancement.  
3. Edge cases applied: if a policy emphasizes circular material use or waste minimization, those could sometimes resemble Resource Efficiency, but under this canonical mechanism the stated objective and causal chain are explicitly carbon reduction from construction materials, so Mitigation remains primary. If timber substitution is used, carbon storage co-benefits may occur, but the mechanism is still classified by the dominant emissions-reduction pathway.  
4. Applicability across all instances: all policies sharing this mechanism act through lowering embodied emissions from building materials or construction processes, so the same primary category applies across instances.  
5. Secondary subtype assignment is deferred to policy-level evidence: specific subtypes such as procurement, codes, disclosure, or circularity-related implementation should be assigned later based on the individual policy text.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential ambiguity arises when policies combine embodied carbon reduction with broader circular economy or material-efficiency goals. In those cases, Resource Efficiency may be a secondary lens, but for this canonical mechanism the dominant pathway is still emissions reduction from construction materials. Timber or other bio-based material policies should only be reclassified if the mechanism is explicitly framed around ecosystem management or carbon sinks rather than construction emissions reduction.
key_indicators: “design and construction specifications”; “purchasing agreements”; “healthy, low-carbon buildings”; “In partnership with other cities and states”; “develop City of Austin”
co_benefits: Health and occupant wellbeing benefits from “healthy” buildings; possible resilience-related gains such as improved indoor comfort during extreme heat, but these are not explicit enough to warrant a secondary category.
instance_edge_case_notes: Some ambiguity with infrastructure-investment or planning-strategy because the policy involves developing specifications rather than explicitly mandating immediate compliance, but the inclusion of “purchasing agreements” makes procurement-requirement the best fit. “Healthy” could imply non-climate building performance goals, but the climate intent is still explicit through “low-carbon.”
